title: NOC 84121 – Fishing vessel deckhands | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 84121 professionals are the essential frontline workers of Canada’s multi-billion dollar commercial fishing industry. As a TEER 4 occupation, fishing vessel deckhands perform the vital physical and technical tasks required to harvest, sort, and preserve fish and other marine life. In 2026, as Canada continues to expand its "Blue Economy" with a focus on high-value exports like lobster, snow crab, and scallops, these deckhands are in record-high demand. For immigration, this role is a premier entry point through the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) and the new 2026 Sector-Specific Stream for Agriculture and Fish Processing.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level4Major Group84 – Workers in natural resources, agriculture and related productionSub-Major Group841 – Workers in natural resources and fisheriesMinor Group8412 – Workers in agriculture and fishing occupationsNOC Code84121

duties: Gear Operation: Assist in setting, hauling, and stowing various types of fishing gear (e.g., nets, traps, lines) using winches and cranes.
Catch Processing: Sort, clean, and grade fish or shellfish by species and size; stow catch in the vessel's hold with ice or in live tanks.
Vessel Maintenance: Clean and maintain the deck, fishing gear, and vessel equipment; assist with minor mechanical repairs.
Operational Support: Help load supplies, bait, and fuel onto the vessel before departure and assist in docking and undocking.
Safety: Adhere to strict maritime safety protocols, including the use of personal flotation devices (PFDs) and participating in emergency drills.
employment_requirements: No formal education is strictly required, though some secondary school is often preferred.
On-the-Job Training: Extensive practical training is provided by the captain and senior crew members.

Certifications:Marine Emergency Duties (MED) certificate is mandatory for most commercial vessels.First Aid and CPR certification is a standard requirement.A valid Fisherman's Registration Card (issued by the DFO) is required in most provinces.
Marine Emergency Duties (MED) certificate is mandatory for most commercial vessels.
First Aid and CPR certification is a standard requirement.
A valid Fisherman's Registration Card (issued by the DFO) is required in most provinces.
Physical stamina, the ability to work in confined spaces, and a high tolerance for varying weather conditions at sea are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: In the commercial fishing sector, annual earnings often range from $55,000 to $85,000 for successful seasons, depending on the "catch-share" percentage.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate a steady increase in "Value-Added" seafood processing, which requires a consistent supply of high-quality catch from active vessels. The retirement of veteran deckhands and helpers is creating an urgent need for younger, motivated workers, making 2026 a strategic time for international workers to enter the Canadian maritime industry.
labour_market_demand: The demand for fishing vessel deckhands is Very Good, particularly in the Atlantic provinces and British Columbia. In 2026, Nova Scotia, Newfoundland and Labrador, and Prince Edward Island are reporting significant labor shortages as the industry seeks to replace a retiring workforce. With the expansion of year-round fishing seasons in certain regions, Canadian vessel owners are actively utilizing LMIA-backed recruitment to bring in international workers who are prepared for the rigors of life at sea.
High Earning Potential: Many deckhand roles operate on a "catch-share" system, allowing hard-working individuals to earn substantial incomes during peak seasons.
Modern Maritime Experience: Gain hands-on experience with advanced hydraulic hauling equipment and electronic fish-finding technology.
Career Progression: A direct and rapid pathway exists to move into TEER 3 Fisherman/woman (NOC 83121) or TEER 3 Fishing Master (NOC 83120) roles.
Strategic PR Pathways: This occupation is a high-priority "Semi-Skilled" role for coastal provinces looking to stabilize their maritime labor force.
pathways: New 2026 Sector-Specific Stream: IRCC has prioritized Agriculture and Fish Processing as a high-demand sector for targeted PR invitations.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): The fastest and most direct route for maritime workers with a job offer in Atlantic Canada.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Accessible for those working in the province's fishing and aquaculture sectors for at least 9 months.
Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): Targeting workers for maritime-dependent rural communities on the coast.
